recommended_paths: Tighten the TODO before implementation by naming the canonical projection/read-model owner, retiring or converting the current read-time assembler path, and defining a single package-owned refresh/materialization boundary for all producer events.
exact_next_step: Record reviewer resolutions in the governing TODO using the machine-checkable resolution table or equivalent gate ledger, then extract the derived resolution packet and decide whether another bounded review pass is still required.

findings.title: Projection writes lacked one canonical materializer boundary
findings.rationale: The broad trigger set is structurally risky. If each producer implements its own refresh behavior, the projection can diverge by event source, producing stale inviteables or inconsistent card semantics.
findings.suggested_action: Require a single package-owned inviteables projector/materializer with idempotent upsert/delete semantics and source-specific adapters that only call or enqueue that boundary.

findings.title: Canonical GET cutover and old assembler fate were not explicit
findings.rationale: Without a named cutover for the old assembler, implementation can leave both the materialized projection and read-time assembly path alive, creating duplicate old/new paths likely to drift and reintroduce request-time correctness/performance regressions.
findings.suggested_action: Add a required architecture note naming the canonical GET path and the fate of InviteablePeopleService::inviteableItemsFor(), plus tests/guardrails proving GET does not read intermediate sources directly.

findings.title: Presentation mapping owner was not explicit
findings.rationale: A missing mapping owner can lead to repeated UI conditionals or leaking internal states into app cards, even if it is not a release blocker when behavior is tested.
findings.suggested_action: Add a non-blocking implementation note that inviteable card labels should be normalized in one presentation/domain mapping boundary and test that superseded never renders as an exposed internal state.
